summary: Updated player costume/controller accessors and the StaticPlayer costume field signedness so dependent functions in other files could match. The key correction was changing Player_GetCostumeId from s8 to u32 while storing the local and struct field as u8, avoiding signed return behavior where the compiled code expected an unsigned value widened to 32 bits.
what_changed: Changed Player_GetCostumeId implementation return type from s8 to u32 in src/melee/pl/player.c.
Changed Player_GetCostumeId local variable costume_id from s8 to u8 before returning it as u32.
Changed StaticPlayer::costume_id at offset 0x44 from s8 to u8 in src/melee/pl/player.h.
Updated Player_GetCostumeId declaration in player.h from s8 to u32.
Changed Player_GetControllerIndex declaration and definition return type from s8 to u8, while leaving the local controller_index variable and struct field as s8 in the shown diff.
No review comments were present in the provided PR slice.
smart_moves: Synchronized the implementation and header prototypes, preventing call sites from compiling against stale signed return types.
Fixed signedness at the data-field level for costume_id, not only at the function prototype, which makes later matching work less fragile.

Used u32 as the public return type for Player_GetCostumeId while keeping the stored costume value byte-sized, matching the common compiler behavior of returning small unsigned values widened through r3.
Made a minimal focused patch, reducing risk while unblocking matching in other files as stated in the PR body.
decomp_lessons: Accessor return types can need to reflect the widened ABI-visible value rather than just the physical size of the underlying struct field.
If a getter feeds other functions that fail to match, check whether signed extension versus zero extension at the call boundary is responsible.
For byte fields that represent IDs or enum-like non-negative values, u8 may be more accurate than s8 even if both occupy the same struct offset.
Header declarations are part of matching: incorrect signedness in a prototype can affect generated code in unrelated translation units.
Small type-only fixes can be important decompilation progress when they remove mismatches outside the directly edited file.
naming_conventions: Player accessors use the Player_GetX and Player_SetX naming pattern, e.g. Player_GetCostumeId, Player_SetCostumeId, Player_GetControllerIndex, Player_SetControllerIndex.
The static player slot array is named player_slots and indexed after Player_CheckSlot(slot).
Local variables mirror field names such as costume_id and controller_index.
The struct type is named StaticPlayer, with documented offsets in comments such as /*0x44*/ costume_id.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Use unsigned byte types when the expected code path requires zero-extension instead of sign-extension.
A function returning u32 from a u8 local can better model code that loads a byte and returns a zero-extended value in the return register.
When other files fail to match due to a getter call, inspect the callee prototype in the shared header because the caller's generated code may depend on it.
Keep struct layout unchanged while changing signedness when only interpretation, not size or offset, is wrong.
follow_up_queries: Find call sites of Player_GetCostumeId that started matching after the return type changed to u32.
Audit other Player_Get* accessors for signed byte returns that should be u8 or u32.
Check whether StaticPlayer::controller_index should also be u8 or whether only the getter return type needed unsigned behavior.
Search for enum-like player fields stored as s8 that represent non-negative IDs and may require unsigned typing.
